pathophysiology_summary: Ovarian torsion is the partial or complete rotation of the ovary (often with the fallopian tube) around its vascular pedicle, occluding first the low-pressure venous and lymphatic outflow and then the arterial supply. The result is progressive ovarian congestion, oedema, ischaemia and — if unrelieved — infarction and necrosis. It is a time-critical surgical emergency: ovarian viability falls with every hour of ischaemia, so prompt imaging (transvaginal ultrasound with Doppler) and laparoscopic detorsion are needed to salvage the ovary. Sudden onset is classically provoked by movement/exertion or by an enlarging ovary (cyst) that acts as a lead point; the intense visceral pain drives reflex nausea and vomiting. The presentation here is the classic constellation — sudden severe unilateral pelvic pain during exertion, recurrent vomiting, a positional component and an absence of fever — which should raise torsion high on the differential even though definitive confirmation requires imaging and surgery.

differential.evidence_basis: Sudden severe unilateral pelvic pain during exertion with vomiting is the textbook torsion presentation.
Any woman of reproductive age with acute unilateral pelvic pain is ectopic until proven otherwise — must exclude with pregnancy status.
Can also cause sudden unilateral pelvic pain during activity; overlaps with torsion and can coexist as a lead point.
Right-sided classically, but pelvic appendicitis and left-sided mimics must be kept in mind for acute abdomen.
Sudden severe flank-to-groin colic with vomiting — considered and argued against by the anterior pelvic location and absent haematuria.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Early on, the AI Doctor should hold torsion and ectopic simultaneously at the top and drive toward pregnancy status and urgent assessment — not settle on a benign cause.

differential.evidence_basis: Positional exacerbation, constant severe pain and repeated vomiting with no fever fit torsion over an inflammatory or infective cause.
Cannot be excluded on history alone — pregnancy testing is mandatory before it is downgraded.
Considered for pelvic pain but argues against given the abrupt exertional onset and the absence of fever, discharge or a subacute course.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Absence of fever and the abrupt exertional onset shift weight away from PID/appendicitis and toward an acute ischaemic/mechanical ovarian event, but ectopic must remain live until pregnancy is excluded.

differential.evidence_basis: Transvaginal ultrasound showing an asymmetrically enlarged ovary (>4 cm) with reduced or absent Doppler flow is the confirmatory finding; a negative pregnancy test removes ectopic.
Excluded by a negative urine pregnancy test with a regular menstrual history.

differential_progression.scoring_note: These investigations (transvaginal ultrasound, pregnancy test) are ED/clinician resources — the telehealth AI Doctor's job is to route the patient to them urgently, not to obtain them.

differential.evidence_basis: Classic tetrad plus imaging-confirmable ischaemia; time-critical surgical emergency requiring laparoscopic detorsion to salvage the ovary.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Definitive diagnosis and treatment are surgical; the correct telehealth outcome is urgent escalation with the patient kept nil-by-mouth for probable surgery.

red_flags.description: Sudden severe unilateral pelvic pain in a woman of reproductive age — possible ovarian torsion (ischaemic surgical emergency)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Torsion strangulates the ovarian blood supply; ovarian viability falls with every hour of delay to detorsion.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Irreversible ovarian ischaemia and necrosis, loss of the ovary, and risk of peritonitis if the necrotic ovary ruptures.

red_flags.description: Acute unilateral pelvic pain in a potentially pregnant woman — ectopic pregnancy must be excluded
red_flags.clinical_significance: A ruptured ectopic is immediately life-threatening; pregnancy status must be established before any reassurance.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed ectopic can rupture and cause fatal intra-abdominal haemorrhage.

red_flags.description: Intractable pain with persistent vomiting and inability to mobilise
red_flags.clinical_significance: Signals a severe acute abdomen; the patient cannot safely self-manage or reliably self-transport and needs urgent in-person assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delayed surgical assessment, dehydration, and progression of ischaemia.

red_flags.description: Sudden complete relief of pain followed by fever and abdominal distension (late sign of necrosis / rupture)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Painless interval after severe torsion pain can indicate the ovary has infarcted; subsequent fever and distension suggest necrosis and peritonitis.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed peritonitis / sepsis from a necrotic, ruptured ovary.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Dismissing severe acute pelvic pain as period pain, ovulation pain, gastroenteritis or a urinary infection and advising watchful waiting — the commonest way torsion is missed, and it costs the ovary.
Not asking about last menstrual period and pregnancy possibility, and not recommending a pregnancy test — a missed ectopic is immediately life-threatening.
Assuming a surgical abdomen must be right-sided (appendicitis) and under-weighting a left-sided ischaemic gynaecological cause.
Attempting to diagnose or manage remotely (or waiting for the vomiting to settle) instead of routing urgently to imaging/surgery — torsion cannot be confirmed or treated by chat.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Abdominal palpation findings — localized guarding and rigidity in the left iliac fossa, marked tenderness on deep palpation
Bimanual pelvic examination — a highly tender palpable left adnexal mass
Costovertebral angle tenderness (assessed absent on examination)
Measured vital signs (blood pressure, heart rate, temperature, respiratory rate, oxygen saturation)
Transvaginal ultrasound with Doppler — an asymmetrically enlarged ovary >4 cm with absent/decreased arterial and venous flow
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a possible time-critical gynaecological surgical emergency and arrange urgent emergency-department assessment for a transvaginal ultrasound and probable surgery; call an ambulance (000) if the patient cannot be transported promptly and safely given severe pain and persistent vomiting. Keep the patient nil-by-mouth in anticipation of surgery.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The AI Doctor cannot confirm torsion (needs ultrasound/surgery) and must not label it benign. The real risk is under-action — reassuring or delaying rather than escalating.

guidelines_referenced.reference: RANZCOG / eTG complete — acute pelvic pain and suspected ovarian torsion
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Suspected ovarian torsion is a surgical emergency; arrange urgent transvaginal ultrasound with Doppler and gynaecological surgical review without delay, and exclude pregnancy in any woman of reproductive age with acute pelvic pain.
